title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
If India redraws its parliamentary map by population, the states losing seats are the ones that cut their birth rates, schooled their daughters, and grew richer. The same states already lost money when the Finance Commission switched to a newer census. The penalty for governing well is double.


Piyush Zaware · Northwestern Kellogg / University of Chicago



543 Seats Frozen Since 1971


South Loses 26


UP +11 · Bihar +10


r = −0.70


4 Apportionment Methods

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
section: The Defeat That Froze the Map
text:
The Defeat That Froze the Map
In a special session in April 2026, the government introduced three bills to settle one of the most consequential questions in Indian federalism: how to redraw the boundaries and the seat counts of the Lok Sabha after half a century of freeze. The centerpiece, the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill, would have expanded the house from 543 seats to 850. It needed a two-thirds majority. It did not get one. The bills lapsed within two days.
The defeat settled nothing. It only postponed the reckoning and left the existing map in place: a map drawn on the 1971 census, when India had roughly 548 million people. The country now has more than 1.4 billion. The freeze was meant to be temporary, a way to reassure states that controlled their populations that they would not lose parliamentary weight for doing so. That promise has been extended again and again. Each extension makes the eventual correction larger and more explosive.
This piece does not take a side on whether the freeze should end. It asks a narrower, answerable question: if India does reapportion by population, who gains, who loses, and what do the losers have in common?

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
The Map Is a 1971 Photograph
Start with what the freeze has already produced. Because seats were fixed while populations kept changing, the number of people behind each seat now varies enormously from state to state.



Population (2026 projection) per current Lok Sabha seat, by state. The dashed line is the national average. Northern states sit above it (under-represented); the South sits below (over-represented). Sources: NCP 2020 projections; current seat allocation.


A voter in Rajasthan or Bihar today sits behind roughly 3.3 million people per seat. A voter in Kerala sits behind about 1.8 million, and in Tamil Nadu about 2 million. That gap is not a plan or a policy. It is the mechanical residue of holding seats constant while some states grew faster than others. By the standard measure of malapportionment, about 8.5 percent of Lok Sabha representation is now out of place relative to population, the equivalent of roughly 46 seats sitting in the wrong state.
This is the part both sides of the debate agree on. One person’s vote should carry roughly the same weight as another’s, and right now it does not. The disagreement begins when you ask how to fix it.

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
Who Gains, Who Loses
The simplest fix, and the principle written into the defeated amendment, is to reallocate the existing 543 seats so that every seat holds roughly the same number of people. Here is what that does.



Projected change in Lok Sabha seats if 543 seats are reapportioned by 2026 population (Webster method). Blue = Hindi-belt, red = South, grey = rest. Sources: computed from NCP 2020 projections.


Uttar Pradesh gains 11 seats. Bihar gains 10. Rajasthan gains 7 and Madhya Pradesh 5. On the other side, Tamil Nadu loses 10, Kerala 6, Andhra Pradesh 5, West Bengal and Odisha 4 each. The five southern states together lose 26 seats. Forty-one seats move in total.
There is nothing dishonest in this reapportionment. It is exactly what “one person, one vote” requires. A Tamil voter and a Bihari voter would, after it, carry the same weight in Delhi. The problem is what the map looks like once you notice which states are on which side.

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
The Performance Penalty
Here is the uncomfortable pattern. Sort the states not by region but by how they performed on the goals that national development policy has pursued for fifty years: lower fertility, female education, rising incomes. The states that did best are the states that lose seats.



Each major state’s projected seat change plotted against its fertility rate. Point size is population. Southern states (red) cluster at low fertility and seat loss; the Hindi-belt (blue) clusters at high fertility and seat gain. Sources: seat change computed (Webster) from NCP 2020 projections; TFR from NFHS-5.


The relationship is steep and clean. A state’s total fertility rate alone predicts its seat change with a correlation of +0.66: the more children per woman, the more seats it gains. Tamil Nadu and Kerala brought their fertility below replacement decades ago, ran some of India’s best public health and schooling systems, and sit at the bottom of the chart. Uttar Pradesh and Bihar, with the highest fertility and the weakest human development indicators, sit at the top.
It is not only fertility. The same pattern holds for female literacy.



Projected seat change plotted against female literacy. The relationship runs the wrong way for fairness: the more a state educated its women, the more seats it stands to lose. Sources: seat change (Webster) from NCP 2020 projections; female literacy from Census 2011.


Combine fertility, female literacy, and per-capita income into a single development index, and the correlation with seat change is −0.70. In a regression across the 21 major states, development explains 48 percent of the variation in seat change, and the relationship is significant at well below the one-in-a-thousand level. This is not a story about the South as a region. It is a story about performance. The states being asked to give up parliamentary voice are, almost exactly, the states that delivered what the country said it wanted.






Important



Why this is not just “the South complaining.” The southern states are over-represented today and a population-based correction would reduce that. That much is fair on its own terms. The deeper point is the incentive it sets. A state that lowers its fertility, educates its girls, and slows its population growth ends up with less national political power than a state that does none of those things. Whatever one thinks about the freeze, that is a strange thing for a country to reward.

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
It Is Not the Formula
One natural objection: maybe this is an artifact of the particular apportionment method. Countries use different rules to turn population into seats, and the choice can favor large or small states at the margin. So we ran the reapportionment four ways: Webster (the standard, also used in recent think-tank analyses), Jefferson, Huntington-Hill (the method the United States uses for its House), and Hamilton.



Projected seat change for the five southern states under four standard apportionment methods. The choice of mathematics changes almost nothing. Sources: methods computed from NCP 2020 projections.


The South loses the same 26 seats under all four. Tamil Nadu loses 10 in every method, Kerala 6, Andhra Pradesh 5. The reason is simple: the population gaps between states are so large that no reasonable rounding rule can bridge them. The result is driven by demography, not by the arithmetic chosen to express it.

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
section: The Penalty Is Double: Seats and Money
text:
The Penalty Is Double: Seats and Money

The same states lose at the Finance Commission too
So far this has been a story about seats. But parliamentary voice is not the only thing India allocates by population. Every five years the Finance Commission decides how the central tax pool is divided among the states, and in 2021 the Fifteenth Finance Commission changed its formula in exactly the way delimitation would: it dropped the 1971 population baseline and used the 2011 population instead. The effect was the same, and it fell on the same states.



Projected Lok Sabha seat change plotted against the change in tax-devolution share between the 14th and 15th Finance Commissions. The southern states fall in the bottom-left quadrant: they lose both. Sources: seat change from NCP 2020 projections (Webster); devolution shares from PRS / 15th Finance Commission report.


Put each state’s seat change on one axis and its fiscal-share change on the other, and the southern states drop into the corner that loses both. Karnataka’s devolution share fell by a full percentage point, from 4.71 to 3.65. Kerala’s fell from 2.50 to 1.93. Andhra Pradesh and Telangana lost ground too. Across the five southern states the loss is about 2.2 percentage points of the divisible pool, which over the 2021 to 2026 award period works out to roughly Rs 92,000 crore.
The same demographic success that costs these states seats also costs them money. But the Rs 92,000 crore is the net change between two different formulas, and honesty requires asking how much of it is really the census switch.



State share of the tax-devolution pool, 14th to 15th Finance Commission. The southern states (red) fall; the large northern states (blue) rise. Source: PRS / 15th Finance Commission report, Table 2.




How much is really the census switch?
It is tempting to call the full Rs 92,000 crore a “population penalty,” but that would be wrong. The 15th Finance Commission did not just swap 1971 population for 2011 population. It redesigned the whole formula at once: it cut the weight on the equity (income-distance) criterion, and it added two new criteria, demographic performance and tax effort, that reward exactly the states that lowered their fertility and collect taxes well. Those changes help the South. The Rs 92,000 crore nets all of this together.
So I isolated the census switch alone. The population criterion carries 15 percent weight in the 15th FC formula and is allocated by each state’s share of population. I recomputed that one criterion using 1971 instead of 2011 population, holding every other criterion fixed, which gives the clean, other-things-equal effect of the census-year change.



For each southern state, the net change in tax-devolution money between the 14th and 15th Finance Commissions, split into the pure 1971-to-2011 census switch (dark red) and everything else in the redesign (orange). The census switch is only part of the gap. Source: author’s reconstruction of the 15th FC population criterion; devolution shares from PRS / 15th FC report.


The result reframes the story. Of the South’s Rs 92,000 crore net loss, the pure census switch accounts for only about Rs 34,000 crore, roughly a third. The other two-thirds, close to Rs 58,000 crore, comes from the rest of the redesign, above all the equity criterion, which sends less money to richer states. The southern states lose more because they are rich than because they are populous-on-the-old-count. Karnataka is the extreme case: of its Rs 45,000 crore loss, barely Rs 4,000 crore is the census switch; the rest is the price of having become a high-income state. Tamil Nadu loses Rs 13,000 crore on the census switch but is handed back even more by the equity and performance credits, so on the fiscal side it ends up slightly ahead.


One honest complication
This matters for how hard to push the “double penalty” line. The fiscal penalty is real but it is softer than the seat penalty, and it is mostly an equity effect rather than a population effect. The Finance Commission sends more to poorer states through the income-distance criterion, and that is why a rich state like Tamil Nadu can lose ten seats yet gain fiscal share. Delimitation has no such cushion: seats are allocated on population alone.
The deeper point survives. The instrument that allocates political power, seats, is pure population, and it punishes the South hardest with no offset. The instrument that allocates money, devolution, is population softened by equity, so its population sting is smaller and partly repaid. The direction is the same on both, and the same five states sit on the losing side of each, but only the seat penalty is a clean population penalty. The fiscal one is mostly the cost of having grown rich.

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
section: The Trap With No Clean Exit
text:
The Trap With No Clean Exit
If reapportioning by population punishes the states that governed best, why not just expand the house so nobody loses a seat? That was the logic of the defeated 850-seat bill. It is the most tempting option and the most quietly dishonest.
Expansion lets every state gain seats in absolute terms, which is why it is politically attractive. But share is what determines power, and in share terms expansion that preserves the current proportions simply freezes the 1971 distortion in a larger house. The South keeps its seats but watches its slice of a bigger pie shrink anyway, because the new seats are handed out in proportions set when India looked very different. Expansion does not resolve the tension. It hides it.
That is the trap. Every option on the table breaks something:

Reapportion by population and you penalize the states that did what was asked, and you concentrate national power in the Hindi-belt.
Expand the house to protect seats and you entrench a half-century-old distortion while pretending to fix it.
Keep the freeze and the malapportionment festers, with each passing year making the eventual correction larger and the gap between a vote in Bihar and a vote in Kerala wider.


There is no formula that makes this fair. The honest conclusion from the data is that delimitation in India is not a technical problem waiting for the right apportionment method. It is a values problem. The country has to decide what it is willing to trade between two principles it has long claimed to hold at once: that every vote should count equally, and that a federation should not punish its members for governing well. The April 2026 defeat did not resolve that choice. It deferred it.

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
The Data
The analysis rests on public, official sources, and the numbers reconcile against known totals.







Source
What it provides




Census of India 2011
State populations (base year)


National Commission on Population (2020)
State population projections, 2026 and 2036


Election Commission of India
Current Lok Sabha seat allocation (543)


NFHS-5 (2019-21)
Total fertility rate by state


Census 2011
Female literacy by state


MOSPI / RBI
Per-capita state income (approximate)


PRS / 15th Finance Commission report
Tax-devolution shares, 14th and 15th FC



The population figures were extracted directly from the National Commission on Population’s 2020 report. As a check, the 36 state and union territory populations sum to 1.211 billion for 2011, matching the Census of India exactly, and Tamil Nadu’s projected growth matches the figure the report itself states.






Important



These are projections, not certainties. The 2026 and 2036 population figures are official projections made in 2020, before the delayed census. No actual delimitation has occurred or can occur until fresh census data exists. Everything here describes what reapportionment on the best available population estimates would do, not what will certainly happen.

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
Methods
Apportionment. Seats are allocated to states by population under four standard methods, each implemented from first principles: Webster (Sainte-Lague), Jefferson (D’Hondt), Huntington-Hill (the US House method), and Hamilton (largest remainder). Every state is guaranteed at least one seat. The headline figures use Webster, the most widely used standard and the method behind recent independent analyses.
The performance penalty. Projected seat change is regressed on total fertility rate and on a composite development index, built from the z-scores of negative fertility, female literacy, and log per-capita income. The regression covers the 21 major states (current seats of four or more), so that the smallest union territories, which keep one seat mechanically, do not distort the relationship. The correlation between the development index and seat change is −0.70, significant at p = 0.0003, with an R-squared of 0.48. The result is robust: it holds when Tamil Nadu, the single largest loser, is dropped (−0.72), and the outlier-resistant rank (Spearman) correlation is −0.52. One honest caveat: part of this relationship is the demographic transition working through arithmetic, since lower fertility means slower population growth and seats track population. The contribution here is to measure the size of the penalty, not to claim it is surprising.
Malapportionment. The “out of place” figure (8.5 percent, about 46 seats) is computed directly as one half of the sum of absolute differences between each state’s seat share and its population share, the standard malapportionment index.
Validation. The apportionment engine reproduces independently published estimates: run on 2011 population, it returns the same seat changes reported by PRS Legislative Research for the 2026 Delimitation Bill (Uttar Pradesh +9, Bihar +6, Rajasthan +5, Tamil Nadu −7, Kerala −5).
The double penalty. Tax-devolution shares are the official inter-se shares from the 14th and 15th Finance Commissions (PRS, from the 15th FC report, Table 2). The rupee figure uses the 15th FC states’ pool of about Rs 42.2 lakh crore over 2021 to 2026, so one percentage point of share is roughly Rs 42,200 crore. The seat penalty and the fiscal penalty move together (correlation +0.41). The development-fiscal correlation is weaker than the development-seat correlation, because the Finance Commission formula contains an equity (income-distance) component that partly cushions poorer states, while seat allocation does not.
Isolating the census switch. The Rs 92,000 crore is the net change between two different formulas, so it cannot be read as a pure population penalty. To isolate the 1971-to-2011 census effect, I recompute only the 15th FC population criterion (15 percent weight, allocated by population share) using 1971 instead of 2011 population, holding every other criterion at its 15th FC value; the change in each state’s share is the other-things-equal census effect. Shares are taken over the 28 states in the 15th FC inter-se distribution (Jammu & Kashmir excluded, a Union Territory since 2019). 1971 population is the boundary-consistent census total, with the four reorganized states (Telangana, Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Uttarakhand) apportioned from their 1971 parent total by 2011 proportion; the southern figure is robust to this because only Andhra Pradesh and Telangana are split, and they are checked combined. This attributes about Rs 34,000 crore (a third) of the southern loss to the census switch and the remaining roughly Rs 58,000 crore to the rest of the redesign, mainly the equity criterion penalizing higher-income states.
A full series-by-series provenance audit is in the project repository. The compiled dataset and all code are available on request.

Related reading

Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, “Delimitation After Defeat: India’s Unfinished Debate Over Representation” (2026), the most thorough recent analysis of the scenarios and the malapportionment math.
National Commission on Population, Population Projections for India and States 2011-2036 (2020).

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
About
Piyush Zaware is a researcher at the Global Poverty Research Laboratory, Northwestern Kellogg School of Management, and a doctoral student in economics at the University of Chicago. He works on political economy, public finance, and state capacity in India.
piyushz@uchicago.edu

title: Data Provenance: Delimitation 2026 Analysis
text:
Author: Piyush Zaware Last updated: 2026-06-20
Every series, its source, and its reliability. Same three-tier scheme as the farmer-suicides project.









Series
Tier
Source
Verification




State population 2011
A
Census of India 2011
Sum across 36 states/UTs = 1,211,127 thousand, matches India 2011 census (1.21 billion) exactly


State population 2026, 2036
A
National Commission on Population, Population Projections for India and States 2011-2036 (2020), Table 8/21, total persons, ’000
Extracted directly from the report PDF. Tamil Nadu 2011-36 growth (+8%) matches the report’s own stated figure; every 2011 value matches census


Current Lok Sabha seats
A
Election Commission of India / Lok Sabha
Sum = 543 elected seats; verified by hand


Total Fertility Rate
A
NFHS-5 (2019-21) state fact sheets
Standard published values


Female literacy
A
Census 2011
Standard published values


Per-capita NSDP
C
MOSPI / RBI Handbook of Statistics on Indian States (approx, 2011-12 constant)
Approximate, used only for relative ranking in the composite development index. Not a headline number


Tax-devolution shares (14th, 15th FC)
A
PRS Legislative Research, from the official 15th Finance Commission report, Table 2
Official inter-se shares. Rupee conversion uses the 15th FC states’ pool (~Rs 42.2 lakh crore, 2021-26)






Apportionment: four standard methods implemented from first principles in code/A2_apportion.R: Webster/Sainte-Lague (the standard, also used by Carnegie 2026), Jefferson/D’Hondt, Huntington-Hill (US House), Hamilton/largest remainder. Every state guaranteed at least one seat. Verified: the five southern states lose 26 seats combined under all four methods, matching independent estimates (e.g., P. Chidambaram’s “South loses 26”).
Performance-penalty regression: OLS of projected seat change on TFR and on a composite development index (z-scores of negative TFR, female literacy, log per-capita NSDP), restricted to 21 major states (current seats >= 4) so that min-1-seat UTs do not add mechanical zeros. Result: corr(development index, seat change) = -0.70, p = 0.0003, R-squared = 0.48.





Projections, not certainties. 2026 and 2036 figures are NCP projections from 2020, made before the 2021 census (which has not been conducted). They are the best official numbers available but are projections.
The 2021 census has not happened. Any actual delimitation would use fresh census data that does not yet exist. This analysis uses NCP projections as the best proxy.
Seat counts assume each state keeps >= 1 seat and that delimitation reallocates by population alone, the principle in the (defeated) Constitution 131st Amendment Bill, 2026.
Per-capita NSDP is approximate and enters only the composite index, not any headline number. The TFR and literacy results (Tier A) carry the argument.





Replace NSDP approximations with exact MOSPI state domestic product series.
Add the 848-seat expansion scenario’s effect on vote share (not just absolute seats) to show expansion does not protect the South’s relative voice.
Project the Samuels-Snyder malapportionment index forward to 2041.

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
The Gerrymander One Level Up
Americans have a word for rigging a political map: gerrymandering. It is worth understanding, because India is about to confront a larger version of the same problem, and most readers will not recognise it by that name.
In the United States, the party that controls a state legislature usually draws that state’s electoral boundaries. Two tricks do the work. “Packing” crams the opposition’s voters into a few districts they win by enormous margins, so their votes pile up uselessly. “Cracking” splits them across many districts so they fall just short in each. Draw the lines well and you can win a majority of seats with a minority of votes. The map, not the voter, decides.
India was built to resist exactly this. Constituency boundaries here are not drawn by politicians. They are drawn by an independent Delimitation Commission, headed by a retired Supreme Court judge, whose orders carry the force of law and cannot be questioned in any court. Partisan line-drawing of the American kind has never been one of Indian democracy’s problems, and that is a genuine achievement worth stating plainly.
But the fight of 2026 is not about where the lines go inside a state. It is about how many seats each state gets in the first place, and that is a lever every bit as powerful as an American gerrymander, operating one level up. In the United States, gerrymandering decides which party wins inside a state. In India, delimitation decides which states win inside the union. The American version at least keeps the total fixed. India’s version transfers power wholesale, from the states that slowed their population growth to the states that did not.
Here the levers are not lines but rules, and there are three. The first is the census you count from: freeze the map on 1971, as India has for fifty years, and you silently over-represent the states that have since grown slowly. This is gerrymandering by inaction, and on our numbers it has already misallocated about 46 seats. The second is the formula: as the robustness section showed, whether you apportion by Webster, Jefferson, or Huntington-Hill quietly moves seats between states, the same population yielding different maps through a choice no voter ever sees. The third is who sits on the commission, and whether its independence holds when the stakes are this high.
India does not have to imagine any of this. It happened in 2022. After the central government stripped Jammu and Kashmir of its statehood, a delimitation commission redrew the new union territory’s assembly map. It handed the Jammu region six new seats and the Kashmir valley only one, even though the valley has the larger population.1 Opposition parties called it a gerrymander in plain words: a map drawn to tilt the assembly toward Hindu-majority, BJP-leaning Jammu by under-weighting the more populous, Muslim-majority valley. The commission pointed to terrain and accessibility. Whatever the merits, the episode showed two things at once: that the same institution praised for its neutrality can produce a map one side calls rigged, and that the rule shielding its orders from any court cuts both ways, because there was no judicial forum left to test the charge.
The national reallocation of 2026 is not partisan line-drawing of that kind, and no one is accusing the apportionment of packing or cracking. The point is the deeper one the American example teaches: in any democracy, power is a function of how you draw and count the map, and the largest transfers of power hide inside technical choices that look neutral. India’s question is not which voters to pack. It is which census to count, which formula to trust, and whether the states on the losing side will accept a map that follows every rule and still takes their voice away.

title: India’s Plan to Punish the States That Did Everything Right
text:
Footnotes


Jammu and Kashmir Delimitation Commission final order (Justice (Retd.) Ranjana Prakash Desai, presiding), notified 20 May 2022: a 90-member assembly with 43 seats for the Jammu division and 47 for Kashmir, six of seven newly created seats going to Jammu. By the 2011 census the Kashmir valley (68.88 lakh) is more populous than Jammu (53.5 lakh). The characterisation of the order as gerrymandering is the stated position of opposition parties, principally the National Conference and the PDP, not a finding of this analysis; the commission cited terrain, connectivity, and administrative convenience. Reporting: The Tribune and Business Standard, 20 May 2022.↩︎
